request.question: Mia Hansen-Løve won the Silver Bear for Best Director for a film staring whom?

title: Norbert Kückelmann
45 Years
All Is Forgiven (film)
Things to Come (2016 film)
Father of My Children
Goodbye First Love
Mia Hansen-Løve
Kim Ki-duk
Alice de Lencquesaing
Eden (2014 French film)
sentences: Norbert Kückelmann, (born 1 May 1930 - died 31 August 2017) was a German film director, screenwriter and lawyer.
He was born in Munich, During the 1950s he studied law and worked part-time as a film critic.
After graduation, he worked as a lawyer in Munich and Mainz.
In 1965 he founded together with Alexander Kluge and Hans-Rolf Strobel the Young German Film Committee (German: "Kuratorium Junger Deutscher Film" .
Continuing to work as a lawyer he directed his first film "Die Sachverständigen" in 1973.
At the 23rd Berlin International Film Festival the film won a Silver Bear.
His first film also won the Deutscher Filmpreis - Best Feature Film.
At the 34th Berlin International Film Festival, his film "Man Under Suspicion" also won a Silver Bear.
Two years later, he was a member of the jury at the 36th Berlin International Film Festival.
45 Years is a 2015 British romantic drama film directed and written by Andrew Haigh.
The film is based on the short story "In Another Country" by David Constantine.
The film was screened in the main competition section of the 65th Berlin International Film Festival.
Charlotte Rampling won the Silver Bear for Best Actress and Tom Courtenay won the Silver Bear for Best Actor.
At the 88th Academy Awards, Rampling received a nomination for Best Actress in a Leading Role.
All Is Forgiven (French: Tout est pardonné ) is a 2007 French drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It was screened in the Directors’ Fortnight section of the 2007 Cannes Film Festival.
It won the Louis Delluc Prize for Best First Film.
It was also nominated for the César Award for Best First Feature Film at the 2008 César Awards.
Things to Come (French title: L'Avenir) is a 2016 French-German drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It stars Isabelle Huppert as middle-aged philosophy professor Nathalie Chazeaux, whose life is going through a series of separations.
Father of My Children (French: Le Père de mes enfants ) is a 2009 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It won the Jury Special Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.
It is based in part on the life of the late Humbert Balsan.
Goodbye First Love (French: Un amour de jeunesse ) is a 2011 Franco-German film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It was selected for the main competition section at the 2011 Locarno International Film Festival.
Mia Hansen-Løve (born 5 February 1981) is a French film director, screenwriter and former actress.
Her film "Father of My Children", won the Special Jury Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.
In 2016, she won the Silver Bear for Best Director for her film "Things to Come" at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival.
Kim Ki-duk ( ] ; born December 20, 1960) is a South Korean filmmaker noted for his idiosyncratic art-house cinematic works.
His films have received many distinctions in the festival circuit, making him one of the most important contemporary Asian film directors.

Major festival awards include Golden Lion at 69th Venice International Film Festival for "Pietà", Silver Lion for Best Director at 61st Venice International Film Festival for "3-Iron", Silver bear for Best Director at 54th Berlin International Film Festival for "Samaria" and Un Certain Regard prize at 2011 Cannes Film Festival for Arirang.
His most widely known feature is "Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter... and Spring" (2003), included in film critic Roger Ebert's Great Movies.
Two of his films served as official submissions for Academy award for best foreign language film as South Korean entries.
He has given scripts to several of his former assistant directors including Juhn Jai-hong ("Beautiful" and "Poongsan") and Jang Hoon ("Rough Cut").
Alice de Lencquesaing (born 11 August 1991) is a French actress who appeared in Mia Hansen-Løve's 2009 film "Father of My Children" with her father Louis-Do de Lencquesaing.
She was also in the film "Summer Hours".
Eden is a 2014 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve and co-written with Sven Hansen-Løve.
The film stars Félix de Givry and Pauline Etienne.
The film premiered in the Special Presentations section of the 2014 Toronto International Film Festival.
It was also screened in the 52nd edition of the New York Film Festival and the BFI London Film Festival.
The film's U.S. theatrical release was 19 June 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

Isabelle Huppert | Isabelle Anne Madeleine Huppert (] ; born 16 March 1953) is a French actress who has appeared in more than 110 films since her debut in 1971.  She is the most nominated actress for the César Award, with 16 nominations.  She twice won the César Award for Best Actress, for "La Cérémonie" (1995) and for "Elle" (2016).  Huppert was made Chevalier of the Ordre national du Mérite in 1994 and was promoted to Officier in 2005.  She was made a Chevalier of the Legion of Honour in 1999 and was promoted to Officer in 2009.

Caroline Huppert | Caroline Huppert (born 28 October 1950) is a French film director and screenwriter.  She is the sister of French actress Isabelle Huppert and has directed 20 films since 1977.
